paperTitle: Singapore-Cambridge GCE O-Level 1184 English Language — AI-authored Practice Paper 28: Between Floors (Paper 2 §B style)

sections.instruction: Read the narrative text below about a teenager who is trapped in a faulty HDB lift with an elderly neighbour, and answer the questions that follow. Section B [19 marks] — AI-authored practice paper modelled on the Singapore-Cambridge 1184/02 (formerly 1128) Section B format and calibrated to the SEAB assessment objectives (literal / inferential comprehension, vocabulary in context, language for effect). Original passage written for the morning quiz bank; not from a past exam paper. Items mix mark values [1]/[2] as in the real paper: 10 short-answer questions (Q1-Q10) — 2 literal, 3 vocabulary-in-context, 3 inference, 2 language-for-effect — plus a 4-blank emotion-flowchart MCQ (Q11). Answer in your own words as far as possible.
sections.passage:
Paragraph 1
The lift jolted to a stop somewhere between the seventh and eighth floors, and the lights died with it. For a moment I told myself it was nothing — the lifts in our block had been temperamental for years, ever since the upgrading works — and that the doors would grind open in a second or two. They did not. The darkness pressed in until I could hear only my own breathing, and the cardboard box of groceries in my arms suddenly felt absurdly heavy.

Paragraph 2
Then a voice, dry and unhurried, came from the corner. 'Don't press all the buttons at once,' it said. 'You'll only confuse the poor thing.' I had not even realised someone else was inside with me. As my eyes adjusted to the thin orange glow of the emergency light, I made out an old man — Uncle Roslan from the unit two doors down from ours, the one who watered the corridor plants nobody had asked him to plant.

Paragraph 3
My thumb was still hovering over the alarm button, which I had been jabbing without realising it. 'I called already,' he said. 'Town Council picks up eventually. On a Sunday, maybe twenty minutes.' Twenty minutes. The number sat in my chest like a stone. I lowered myself to the floor beside the grocery box because my legs had begun, very quietly, to shake.

Paragraph 4
'First time stuck?' he asked. I nodded, then remembered he probably could not see me, and said yes. He chuckled — not unkindly. 'Forty years I've lived in this block,' he said. 'The lift and I are old enemies. It has caught me eleven times. Twelve now, but tonight I have company.' Somehow the way he counted it, as though being trapped were merely an inconvenient appointment, loosened something in my chest.

Paragraph 5
We talked, because there was nothing else to do and because his voice made the dark feel smaller. He asked what was in my box, and when I said it was ingredients for my mother's birthday dinner — I was supposed to be cooking it that very evening — he was quiet for a while. 'Your mother is lucky,' he said at last. 'My children call on birthdays. From Perth, from Toronto. Good children. But a phone call you cannot eat.' He said it lightly, but the lightness had a weight beneath it that I had not expected, and I did not know what to say, so I said nothing, which I think was the right thing.

Paragraph 6
When I told him I was worried the chicken would spoil before we got out, he laughed properly for the first time. 'In this heat? Cook it tonight, it will be fine. In my day we had no fridge at all. We hung things from the ceiling so the cats couldn't reach.' And he told me about the kampung he had grown up in before the flats came, about the well and the rambutan tree and the neighbour who could whistle like a bird, until the twenty minutes had somehow folded in on themselves and I realised I was no longer counting them.

Paragraph 7
The lift shuddered. The lights flared back, white and sudden, and we both winced. Through the small window the floor indicator blinked: 7. The doors dragged themselves open. A Town Council man in a yellow vest peered in. 'Everybody okay?' Uncle Roslan got up slowly, one hand on the rail, and offered the other to me. 'Up you get,' he said. 'The chicken is waiting.'

Paragraph 8
We parted at the seventh-floor lift lobby, he to wait for the next car down, I to climb the last flight to my own unit. At my door I turned. He was still standing there, a small figure under the corridor light, the corridor plants he had not been asked to plant trailing green over the parapet behind him. 'Uncle,' I called, 'come for dinner. My mother would like that.' For a second he said nothing. Then he raised one hand — not quite a wave, more an acknowledgement — and I understood that he would come, and that the lift, for once, had caught me at exactly the right time.

(AI-authored original narrative for O-Level 1184 §B style practice; not from a past examination paper. HDB: Housing and Development Board, Singapore's public housing; Town Council: the body that maintains common areas of a public housing estate; kampung: a traditional Malay village; upgrading works: government-funded renovations to older public housing blocks.)

questions.stem: Q1. From Paragraph 1, between which two floors did the lift jolt to a stop? [1]
questions.answer: between the seventh and eighth floors

questions.stem: Q2. What does the word 'temperamental' (Paragraph 1) suggest about the lifts in the block? [1]
Q3. What does the word 'unhurried' (Paragraph 2) suggest about the way Uncle Roslan speaks? [1]
questions.answer: calm and relaxed / in no rush

questions.stem: Q4. What does the word 'hovering' in 'My thumb was still hovering over the alarm button' (Paragraph 3) suggest about the narrator's state? [1]
questions.answer: anxious / tense, poised to act on edge

questions.stem: Q5. From Paragraph 4, how many times had the lift caught Uncle Roslan before that night? [1]
Q6. What is the effect of the phrase 'The number sat in my chest like a stone' (Paragraph 3)? [2]
questions.answer: ① the simile likens the twenty-minute wait to a heavy stone; ② making the narrator's dread feel physical and weighing him down

questions.stem: Q7. Using your own words, explain why the narrator lowered himself to the floor in Paragraph 3. [2]
questions.answer: ① his legs had begun to shake; ② from fear at the thought of being trapped for twenty minutes

questions.stem: Q8. Using your own words, explain what Uncle Roslan means by 'a phone call you cannot eat' (Paragraph 5). [2]
questions.answer: ① his children only phone him from overseas; ② a call cannot replace their physical presence and sharing a meal, hinting at his loneliness

questions.stem: Q9. Using your own words, explain why the narrator felt that saying nothing 'was the right thing' (Paragraph 5). [2]
questions.answer: ① he sensed the real sadness beneath Uncle Roslan's light tone; ② any reply would have been inadequate or intrusive, so silence showed respect and sympathy

questions.stem: Q10. What is the effect of the phrase 'the twenty minutes had somehow folded in on themselves and I realised I was no longer counting them' (Paragraph 6)? [2]
questions.answer: ① it shows the narrator so absorbed in the old man's stories that he loses track of time; ② his dread has been replaced by interest, so the wait seems to fly by

sections.instruction: Q11. The narrator's dominant feeling shifts as he is trapped in the lift and then freed. For each part of the passage, choose ONE word from the bank that best describes his dominant feeling at that moment. There are some extra words in the bank you do not need to use. Word bank: panic / steadied / connected / grateful / trapped / annoyed / proud / indifferent. The original task carries 4 marks; each blank is split into its own MCQ so each sub-part can be auto-graded independently.
sections.passage: Refer to the same 'Between Floors' narrative in Exercise 1 above. The narrator's feelings shift as the lift breaks down — from panic in the dark as the lights die and his legs begin to shake, to steadied as Uncle Roslan's calm, joking manner loosens the fear in his chest, to connected as the two share stories and the twenty minutes fold away unnoticed, ending grateful as he climbs out and invites the old man to his mother's birthday dinner. Pick the single best word from the bank for each moment.

questions.stem: Q11(i). What best describes the narrator's dominant feeling in Paragraphs 1-3, alone in the sudden darkness, jabbing the alarm button as his legs begin to shake at the thought of a twenty-minute wait? [1]
Q11(ii). What best describes the narrator's dominant feeling in Paragraph 4, as Uncle Roslan counts the lift's breakdowns like routine appointments and the fear in his chest begins to loosen? [1]
Q11(iii). What best describes the narrator's dominant feeling in Paragraphs 5-6, as he and Uncle Roslan share stories of birthdays and the old kampung until he stops counting the minutes? [1]

Q11(iv). What best describes the narrator's dominant feeling in Paragraphs 7-8, as the doors open and he invites Uncle Roslan to his mother's birthday dinner, realising the breakdown caught him at exactly the right time? [1]
